subject: Chemistry
title: Ionic Bonds: Stealing Electrons
description: A fast breakdown of Ionic Bonds: Stealing Electrons for builders.

hook.text: Table salt forms when sodium gives an electron to chlorine. The charged atoms attract and lock into a crystal.

buildup.text: Sodium has one outer electron it wants to lose. Chlorine needs one more electron to fill its outer shell.

discovery.text: Sodium becomes Na⁺ and chlorine becomes Cl⁻. The opposite charges attract with enormous force—an ionic bond.

twist.text: Ionic compounds form rigid crystal lattices, not molecules. Each Na⁺ is surrounded by six Cl⁻ ions and vice versa.

climax.text: Dissolve salt in water and the ions separate. That's why salt water conducts electricity—free ions carry the current.

punchline.text: Give an electron, take an electron, opposite charges lock together. That's salt.

quiz.question: Why does salt dissolve in water and conduct electricity?
options: Water separates Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, which carry electrical current
Salt molecules break apart into individual atoms
Water chemically reacts with salt to produce electrons
